code: library(devtools) install_github("janhove/cannonball")

title: Tools for Teaching Statistics
code: # Load the package library(cannonball)

what: plot_r(): Draw different scatterplots with the same correlation coefficient
title: Tools for Teaching Statistics
text: single correlation coefficient can correspond number scatterplots. plot_r() produces 16 scatterplots given Pearson correlation coefficient drive point home.  info, see ?plot_r. Accompanying blog post: data patterns can lie behind correlation coefficient?

title: Tools for Teaching Statistics
text: help students see connection experiment’s design analysis, ’ve written two functions. walkthrough_p() guides user completely randomised experiment: Data points generated randomly assigned either control intervention condition. , intervention effect added data points intervention condition. Finally, data analysed using Student’s t-test , pedant = TRUE, permutation test. walkthrough_blocking() works similarly walkthrough_p() describes randomised block design: Prior information data points available form covariate (e.g., pretest score). information used group participants ‘blocks’, randomisation restricted one participant per block assigned control one intervention condition. Crucially, analysis needs take restricted randomisation account. alternative, powerful, less easily explained analysis use blocking covariate control variable general linear model rather blocking factor.
code: # see ?walkthrough_p walkthrough_p(n = 18, diff = 0.3, sd = 1, pedant = TRUE) # ?walkthrough_blocking walkthrough_blocking(n = 12, diff = 0.4, sd = 1)

title: Tools for Teaching Statistics
text: data experiments entire clusters participants (e.g., classes) assigned experimental conditions can’t analysed way data experiments participants assigned conditions individually. clustered_data() generates data cluster-randomised experiment can used demonstrate increased Type-error rate data analysed using t-tests individual outcomes.  covariate can also created. info, see ?clustered_data. Accompanying article: Vanhove, Jan. 2015. Analyzing randomized controlled interventions: Three notes applied linguists. Studies Second Language Learning Teaching 5(1). 135–152. (See correction note paper.) Accompanying blog post: Experiments intact groups: spurious significance improperly weighted t-tests.
code: # Simulate clustered data with an ICC of 0.3 d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.3)  # Plot library(ggplot2) ggplot(data = d,        aes(x = reorder(class, outcome, FUN = median),            y = outcome)) +   geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +   geom_point(shape = 1,              position = position_jitter(width = 0.1, height = 0)) +   facet_wrap(~ condition, scales = "free_x") +   xlab("class")

title: Tools for Teaching Statistics
text: functions may helpful helping judge whether data conform assumptions statistical model. embedding model’s diagnostic plot line-diagnostic plots simulated data model’s assumptions literally met, analysts can easily determine whether blips plots indicative assumption violations whether can plausibly accounted sampling error/noise. See Buja et al. (2009).  Related functions : var_plot(): Check non-constant variance residuals. norm_qq(): Check non-normality residuals (quantile–quantile plot). norm_hist(): Check non-normality residuals (histogram). parade_summary(): Summarise residuals per cell (combination unique predictor values). Accompanying article: Vanhove, Jan. 2018. Checking assumptions statistical model without getting paranoid. Preprint PsyArxiv.
code: # Fit model m <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)  # Create parade/line-up my_parade <- parade(m) #> Loading required package: tidyverse #> ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ── #> ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5 #> ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1 #> ✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tibble    3.2.1 #> ✔ purrr     1.0.2     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1 #> ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ── #> ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter() #> ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag() #> ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors # Check for residual nonlinearities: # Which plot looks most different from the rest? lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' # Reveal position of actual diagnostic plot reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 14.

what: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
text: educational settings, often impossible randomly assign individual students one experiment's conditions. Instead, students class school assigned condition. type random assignment known cluster randomisation. Importantly, analyse data cluster-randomised experiment though participants assigned randomly individual basis (e.g., using t-test individual outcomes), Type-error rate (false positive) goes roof. illustrate , function simulates data cluster-randomised experiment. Covariate scores related outcome (referred pretest scores ) can also generated.

title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
code: clustered_data(   n_per_class = rep(20, 10),   ICC = 0.15,   effect = 0,   rho_prepost = NULL,   reliability_post = 1,   reliability_pre = NULL )

title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
text: n_per_class number participants per class. first half classes assigned control condition, second half intervention condition. ICC intra-class correlation. ICC 1 means observations class identical ; ICC 0 means observations within class alike observations different classes. Defaults 0.15. effect population-level effect intervention. Defaults 0. rho_prepost desired population-level correlation measured pretest scores outcomes. reliability_post reliability outcomes. Default 1, meaning measured error outcome. reliability_pre reliability pretest scores.

title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
text: function works follows. within-cluster variance participants' latent pre-intervention skill set 1. within-cluster variance computed basis desired ICC. , pre- posttest measurements generated adding random normal measurement error participants' latent pre-intervention skill. variance measurement error computed basis desired correlation pretest pre-intervention outcome scores; case, reliabilities pretest outcome scores set equal. correlation specified, measurement error computed basis desired pretest posttest reliabilities. Finally, constant intervention effect added outcome scores participants intervention condition.
code: Note that the square root of the product of the pre- and posttest reliabilities yields      the correlation between them. So if you want to have a correlation between pretest and posttest      scores (without an intervention effect) of 0.7 and you want your pretest to be correlated      at 0.9 to the pre-intervention latent skill levels (i.e., have a reliability of 0.9),      then solve \eqn{\sqrt{0.9x} = 0.7} for \eqn{x} (\eqn{x = 0.7^2/0.9 = 0.544}).

title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
text: Vanhove, Jan. 2015. Analyzing randomized controlled interventions: Three notes applied linguists. Studies Second Language Learning Teaching 5(1). 135-152. http://doi.org/10.14746/ssllt.2015.5.1.7. (Correction note: http://pressto.amu.edu.pl/index.php/ssllt/article/view/5827/5895.)

title: Simulate data for a cluster-randomised experiment — clustered_data
code: # Generate and plot data with ICC = 0 d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0) library(tidyverse) #> ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ── #> ✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0 #> ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0 #> ✔ ggplot2   4.0.3     ✔ tibble    3.3.1 #> ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2 #> ✔ purrr     1.2.2      #> ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ── #> ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter() #> ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag() #> ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors ggplot(data = d,        aes(x = reorder(class, outcome, FUN = median),            y = outcome)) +   geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +   geom_point(shape = 1,              position = position_jitter(width = 0.2)) +   facet_wrap(~ condition, scales = "free_x") +   xlab("outcome")   # Generate and plot data with ICC = 0.2 d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.2) ggplot(data = d,        aes(x = reorder(class, outcome, FUN = median),            y = outcome)) +   geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +   geom_point(shape = 1,              position = position_jitter(width = 0.2)) +   facet_wrap(~ condition, scales = "free_x") +   xlab("outcome")   # Generate and plot data with ICC = 0.6 d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.6) ggplot(data = d,        aes(x = reorder(class, outcome, FUN = median),            y = outcome)) +   geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +   geom_point(shape = 1,              position = position_jitter(width = 0.2)) +   facet_wrap(~ condition, scales = "free_x") +   xlab("outcome")   # Simulate 1000 datasets and analyse them using t-test ps <- replicate(1000,                {                  d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.2)                  t.test(outcome ~ condition, data = d)$p.value                }) hist(ps, breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.05))  mean(ps < 0.05) #> [1] 0.387 # Note the hugely inflated Type-I error rate!  # Generate pretest scores with correlation 0.7 to outcome # and with ICC of latent pre-intervention skill of 0.4 d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.4, rho_prepost = 0.7) #> 'rho_prepost' was set, so the 'reliability_pre' and 'reliability_post' were ignored, and both were set to the 'rho_prepost' value. with(d, cor(pretest, outcome)) #> [1] 0.6804858  # ICC = 0.2, pretest reliability = 0.9, posttest reliability = 0.544 # and hence correlation = sqrt(0.9*0.544) =~ 0.7: d <- clustered_data(ICC = 0.2, reliability_pre = 0.9, reliability_post = 0.544) with(d, cor(pretest, outcome)) #> [1] 0.6990089

title: Draw diagnostic parades — diagnostic_plot
text: short-hand functions quickly draw diagnostic parades. lin_plot() plots models' residuals (default) corresponding fitted values. residuals can also plotted specific predictor predictor parameter set. function useful checking linearity assumption. var_plot() plots models' absolute residuals (default) corresponding fitted values fed object generated using parade(). absolute residuals can also plotted specific predictor predictor argument set. fed object generated using parade_summary(), plots sample standard deviation residuals per cell. function useful checking constant-variance assumption. norm_qq() norm_hist() plot normal quantile-quantile plots histograms models' residuals, respectively. function useful checking normality assumption.

title: Draw diagnostic parades — diagnostic_plot
code: lin_plot(parade, predictor = NULL, rank = FALSE)  var_plot(parade, predictor = NULL, rank = FALSE)  norm_qq(parade)  norm_hist(parade, bins = 30)

title: Draw diagnostic parades — diagnostic_plot
text: parade name object generated using parade(). var_plot(), objects generated using parade_summary() also accepted. predictor name variable parade object residuals plotted. parameter specified (default), residuals plotted respective fitted values. rank values along x-axis converted ranks (TRUE) (FALSE, default)? used, ties broken randomly. may useful raw values concentrated certain regions along x-axis, making difficult discern relevant patterns. bins many bins histograms contain? Defaults 30.

title: Draw diagnostic parades — diagnostic_plot
code: # A simple regression model m <- lm(mpg ~ disp, data = mtcars)  # Generate parade and check linearity my_parade <- parade(m) lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 8.  # Regenerate parade and check constant variance my_parade <- parade(m) var_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 4.  # Regenerate parade and check normality my_parade <- parade(m) norm_qq(my_parade)  norm_hist(my_parade)  norm_hist(my_parade, bins = 10)  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 16.  # Example with gam library(mgcv) #> Loading required package: nlme #>  #> Attaching package: ‘nlme’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:dplyr’: #>  #>     collapse #> This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'. m.gam <- gam(mpg ~ s(disp) + wt + s(qsec, by = am), data = mtcars) my_parade <- parade(m.gam) lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  lin_plot(my_parade, predictor = "wt") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  lin_plot(my_parade, predictor = "qsec") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

what: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
text: function generates parade (= lineup nullabor package) hides observations, fitted values, residuals statistical model want diagnose among observations, fitted values, residuals number similar models fitted simulated outcome data. sets simulated outcome data generated original model model's assumptions literally true simulated data. 'tibble' (dataframe) created can used draw panels diagnostic plots (see examples).

title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
code: parade(model, full_data = NULL, size = 20)

title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
text: model name statistical model want diagnose. Currently lm(), gam() (mgcv package) lmer() (lme4 package) models supported. lmer() models, residual diagnostics supported; support BLUP ('random effects') diagnostics still lacking. full_data default, output include variables part model. want include variables present dataframe model fitted, supply dataframe's name full_data. size number simulated actual datasets parade contain. defaults 20, meaning actual dataset hidden among 19 simulated datasets.

title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
text: tibble containing predictors, outcomes, fitted values residuals real dataset simulated datasets.

title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
text: want include transformed predictors model call (e.g., log(x)), transform predictor using model call (see examples). function relies augment broom package. Since augment() handle model calls poly() ns(), parade() handle , either. (lmer models, augment function broom.mixed package used.)

title: Generate dataset for a diagnostic parade — parade
code: # A simple regression model m <- lm(mpg ~ disp, data = mtcars)  # Generate parade and check linearity my_parade <- parade(m) my_parade #> # A tibble: 640 × 7 #>     disp   mpg .fitted  .resid .abs_resid .sqrt_abs_resid .sample #>    <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl>           <dbl>   <int> #>  1  160   24.9    23.3  1.64       1.64             1.28        1 #>  2  160   19.6    23.3 -3.71       3.71             1.93        1 #>  3  108   29.7    25.5  4.22       4.22             2.05        1 #>  4  258   18.6    19.1 -0.485      0.485            0.696       1 #>  5  360   13.9    14.7 -0.770      0.770            0.877       1 #>  6  225   22.6    20.5  2.10       2.10             1.45        1 #>  7  360   14.6    14.7 -0.0989     0.0989           0.315       1 #>  8  147.  22.9    23.9 -0.949      0.949            0.974       1 #>  9  141.  21.7    24.1 -2.44       2.44             1.56        1 #> 10  168.  18.6    23.0 -4.40       4.40             2.10        1 #> # ℹ 630 more rows lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 5.  # Regenerate parade and check constant variance my_parade <- parade(m) var_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 20.  # Regenerate parade and check normality my_parade <- parade(m) norm_qq(my_parade)  norm_hist(my_parade)  norm_hist(my_parade, bins = 10)  reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 1.  # If you want to include all predictors in the dataset in the parade: my_parade <- parade(m, full_data = mtcars) my_parade #> # A tibble: 640 × 16 #>      cyl  disp    hp  drat    wt  qsec    vs    am  gear  carb   mpg .fitted #>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1     6  160    110  3.9   2.62  16.5     0     1     4     4 16.8     22.0 #>  2     6  160    110  3.9   2.88  17.0     0     1     4     4 14.4     22.0 #>  3     4  108     93  3.85  2.32  18.6     1     1     4     1 26.6     24.0 #>  4     6  258    110  3.08  3.22  19.4     1     0     3     1 13.8     18.2 #>  5     8  360    175  3.15  3.44  17.0     0     0     3     2  8.69    14.2 #>  6     6  225    105  2.76  3.46  20.2     1     0     3     1 17.9     19.5 #>  7     8  360    245  3.21  3.57  15.8     0     0     3     4 21.9     14.2 #>  8     4  147.    62  3.69  3.19  20       1     0     4     2 17.4     22.5 #>  9     4  141.    95  3.92  3.15  22.9     1     0     4     2 17.6     22.7 #> 10     6  168.   123  3.92  3.44  18.3     1     0     4     4 24.1     21.7 #> # ℹ 630 more rows #> # ℹ 4 more variables: .resid <dbl>, .abs_resid <dbl>, .sqrt_abs_resid <dbl>, #> #   .sample <int>  # If you want to generate a parade with 50 instead of 20 plots: my_parade <- parade(m, size = 50) norm_qq(my_parade)   # The function also works for generalised additive models fitted with mgcv: library(mgcv) m.gam <- gam(mpg ~ s(disp) + wt + s(qsec), data = mtcars) my_parade <- parade(m.gam) lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  my_parade <- parade(m.gam) norm_qq(my_parade)   m.gam <- gam(mpg ~ te(disp, qsec) + wt, data = mtcars) my_parade <- parade(m.gam) lin_plot(my_parade) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # And has some limited support for lmer() models (from the lme4 package) library(lme4) #> Loading required package: Matrix #>  #> Attaching package: ‘Matrix’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:tidyr’: #>  #>     expand, pack, unpack #>  #> Attaching package: ‘lme4’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:nlme’: #>  #>     lmList m.lmer <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days|Subject), data = sleepstudy) my_parade <- parade(m.lmer) norm_hist(my_parade, bins = 15)  # Support for diagnosing the BLUPs would be nice.  # Transformed predictors: # This won't work: # m <- lm(mpg ~ log2(disp), data = mtcars) # my_parade <- parade(m)  # This will: mtcars$log2.disp <- log2(mtcars$disp) m <- lm(mpg ~ log2.disp, data = mtcars) my_parade <- parade(m)

what: Summarise residual information in a diagnostic parade per cell or per unique predictor value — parade_summary
title: Summarise residual information in a diagnostic parade per cell or per unique predictor value — parade_summary
text: function takes output parade function summarises residuals (fitted values) unique combination variables. may useful checking constant-variance assumption nature data plotting raw residuals make stand distractor plots even non-constant variance much problem. Feed output function var_plot obtain quick diagnostic plot constant-variance assumption. think parade_summary mostly useful dealing fairly discrete outcome data, function throw warning outcome data seem fairly continuous. (Arbitrarily 20 unique outcome values.) also throw warning non-outcome data used define cells categorical number observations per cell seems low (arbitrarily fewer 5 observations). assignment cells design cell numbers parade summary random. , one particular predictor combination may associated Cell 3 running parade_summary() one time, Cell 1 running second time. Within given parade summary, however, cell number always refers combination predictor combinations.

title: Summarise residual information in a diagnostic parade per cell or per unique predictor value — parade_summary
code: parade_summary(parade, predictors_only = FALSE)

title: Summarise residual information in a diagnostic parade per cell or per unique predictor value — parade_summary
text: parade name object generated using parade() function. predictors_only supplied dataset full_data argument parade() call, parade_summary() default compute mean fitted values host residual summaries unique combination non-outcome variables dataset. override behaviour, set predictors_only FALSE; causes parade_summary() compute mean fitted values residual summaries unique combination predictors actually model.

title: Summarise residual information in a diagnostic parade per cell or per unique predictor value — parade_summary
code: # Fit model m <- lm(mpg ~ gear, data = mtcars)  # Generate parade my_parade <- parade(m)  # Summarise residuals by cell - # you'll get some warnings. The second one # because 'gear' is represented as a numeric variable. my_sum_parade <- parade_summary(my_parade) #> Warning: The outcome variable (mpg) contains 25 unique values. Perhaps you can draw standard diagnostic plots instead of averaging the residuals? #> Warning: Not all grouping variables are characters or factors. Are you sure you want to use this function?  # Draw plot var_plot(my_sum_parade)   reveal(my_sum_parade) #> The true data are in position 6. # or reveal(my_parade) #> The true data are in position 6.

title: Draw different scatterplots corresponding to a sample correlation coefficient — plot_r
text: (1) x/y data drawn bivariate normal distribution. Pearson's correlation coefficient can useful situation. (2) x data uniformly distributed 0 1; y data scattered normally regression line. problematic either. (3) x data drawn right-skewed distribution; y data scattered normally regression line. y-data follow data-generating mechanism (.e., outliers reflect process ordinary data points result , say, transcription errors), outlying data points may exert large effect correlation coefficient. (Try exporting dataset plot recomputing correlation coefficient without largest x-value!) (4) (3), time x data drawn left-skewed distribution. (5) x data drawn normal distribution y values right-skewed regression line. Every now one couple datapoints exert large pull correlation coefficient. (6) (5), except y values left-skewed regression line. (7) increasing x values, y values ever scattered regression line. Conceptually, Pearson's correlation coefficients underestimates well can anticipate y-value small x-values overestimates well can large x-values. (8) (7), except scatter regression line becomes smaller larger x-values. (9) Pearson's correlation coefficient summarises linear trend data; trend panel, however, quadratic. result, Pearson's correlation coefficient underestimate strength relationship two variables. (10) Similar (9) trend sinusoid. (11) single positive outlier exerts pull correlation coefficient. contrast (3) (6), outlier caused data-generating mechanism rest data, contaminates estimated correlation coefficient. regression line data without outlier plotted dashed red line; often find true trend data go counter trend outlier excluded. (12) Similar (11), except outlier negative (.e., pulls correlation coefficient ). (13) y data distributed bimodally regression line. suggests important categorical predictor taken account analysis. (14) worrisome version (13). actually two groups data, taken account. within groups, trend may often (always) run counter overall trend. , instance, Pearson's correlation may suggest presence positive correlation, true trend may actually negative – provided group factor taken account. known Simpson's paradox. (15) data panel sampled bivariate normal distribution middle part distribution removed. may sensible sampling strategy want investigate relationship two variables, one expensive measure cheap: Screen large number observations cheap variable, collect expensive variable extreme observations. regression analysis, sampling strategy can highly effective terms power precision. However, artificially inflates correlation coefficients. (16) suspect lots datasets actually look like. x y data categorical. needn't problematic; just someone mentions "r = 0.4", think panel (1) rather panel (16).

what: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
title: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
text: Computes p-values strong null hypothesis differential treatment effect unit.

title: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
code: rand_test(   outcome,   treatment_idx,   block = NULL,   statistic = stud_mean_diff,   exact = TRUE,   plot = TRUE,   M = 20000,   xlab = "test statistic" )

title: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
text: outcome Vector numeric outcomes. treatment_idx Indices observations treatment condition. block using blocking, specifies units blocked together. statistic test statistic two-group comparison (see test_statistics). exact TRUE (default), exhaustive rerandomisation used. Else, Monte Carlo rerandomisation. plot TRUE (default), distribution test statistics H0 plotted. M Number Monte Carlo samples (exact == FALSE). xlab x-axix label histogram showing H0 distribution test statistic.

title: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
text: left-, right, two-sided p-value.

title: Randomisation testing for two-group comparisons — rand_test
code: learners <- data.frame( L1 = c(rep("German", 6), rep("French", 8)), Learner = c(1:6, 1:8) )  learners$Learner <- paste(learners$L1, learners$Learner) learners$MethodB <- round(rnorm(14, mean = 9, sd = 1)) learners$MethodB <- ifelse(learners$L1 == "German",                            learners$MethodB + 3,                            learners$MethodB) differences <- rep(c(2, 1, 3, 0, 4), times = c(8, 3, 3, 0, 0)) |> sample() learners$MethodA <- learners$MethodB - differences my_min <- pmin(min(learners$MethodA, learners$MethodB), 0) learners$MethodA <- learners$MethodA - my_min learners$MethodB <- learners$MethodB - my_min learners$Condition <- c(sample(rep(c(0, 1), each = 3)),                         sample(rep(c(0, 1), each = 4))) learners$Outcome <- learners$Condition * learners$MethodB +   (1 - learners$Condition) * learners$MethodA  # taking blocking into account (exhaustive randomisation) rand_test(learners$Outcome, which(learners$Condition == 1),           block = learners$L1, statistic = mean_diff)  #> $`left-sided p-value` #> [1] 1 #>  #> $`right-sided p-value` #> [1] 0.001428571 #>  #> $`two-sided p-value` #> [1] 0.002857143 #>   # without taking blocking into account rand_test(learners$Outcome, which(learners$Condition == 1),           statistic = mean_diff)  #> $`left-sided p-value` #> [1] 0.9932984 #>  #> $`right-sided p-value` #> [1] 0.01748252 #>  #> $`two-sided p-value` #> [1] 0.03496503 #>

what: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
title: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
text: functions compute test statistics can used randomisation test two-group comparison. mean_diff() computes mean difference. stud_mean_diff() computes studentised mean difference. identical t-statistic used Welch's t-test. median_diff() computes difference medians. rank_sum() computes rank sum treatment group. test statistic used Mann-Whitney-Wilcoxon test. prob_super() computes probability superiority, .e., probability randomly chosen unit treatment group higher outcome value randomly chosen unit control group. resulting test equivalent using rank_sum(). ks_stat() computes Kolmogorov-Smirnov test statistic, .e., supremum difference ecdfs outcome treatment control groups.

title: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
code: mean_diff(outcome, treatment_idx)  stud_mean_diff(outcome, treatment_idx)  median_diff(outcome, treatment_idx)  rank_sum(outcome, treatment_idx)  prob_super(outcome, treatment_idx)  ks_stat(outcome, treatment_idx)

title: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
text: outcome vector numeric outcomes. treatment_idx indices observations treatment group.

title: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
text: test statistic two-group comparison.

title: Test statistics for two-group comparisons — test_statistics
code: x <- c(seq(-4, 5), 2*seq(-3, 4)) g <- rep(c(0, 1), times = c(10, 8)) idx <- which(g == 1) mean_diff(x, idx) #> [1] 0.5 stud_mean_diff(x, idx) #> [1] 0.2526456 median_diff(x, idx) #> [1] 0.5 rank_sum(x, idx) #> [1] 78.5 prob_super(x, idx) #> [1] 0.53125 ks_stat(x, idx) #> [1] 0.25

what: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
title: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
text: function produces step--step demonstration researchers can 'block' continuous covariate can analyse data randomised block design.

title: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
code: walkthrough_blocking(   n = 10,   diff = 0,   sd = 1,   rho = 0.8,   showdata = FALSE,   pedant = FALSE )

title: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
text: n number data points per group. diff boost participants intervention group receive. sd standard deviation normal distributions data drawn. rho correlation covariate outcome (pre-intervention) population. showdata want output dataframe containing plotted data (TRUE) (FALSE, default)? pedant want run significance test pedant mode (TRUE) (FALSE, default)? See Details.

title: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
text: Data generated normal distribution requested standard deviation; covariate also generated. data points grouped pairs based covariate scores. Within pair, data points randomly assigned control intervention group. Data points intervention group receive boost specified 'diff'. Finally, significance test ran data. default, significance test two-sample Student's t-test. Technically, p-value test probability t-statistic larger one observed 've observed chance play, walkthrough text says probability mean difference larger one observed 've observed chance play. , use t-test approximation permutation test. Switch pedant mode want run permutation test.

title: Walkthrough blocking on a covariate — walkthrough_blocking
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ walkthrough_blocking(n = 12, diff = 0.2, sd = 1.3, rho = 0.8, pedant = FALSE)  # Save data and double check results dat <- walkthrough_blocking(n = 12, diff = 0.2, sd = 1.3, rho = 0.8, showdata = TRUE) anova(lm(score ~ factor(block) + group, data = dat))  # Run in pedant mode (= permutation test) walkthrough_blocking(n = 12, diff = 0.2, sd = 1.3, rho = 0.8, pedant = TRUE) } # }

title: Walkthrough p-values — walkthrough_p
text: function produces step--step demonstration significance test two-group comparison.

title: Walkthrough p-values — walkthrough_p
code: walkthrough_p(n = 10, diff = 0, sd = 1, showdata = FALSE, pedant = FALSE)

title: Walkthrough p-values — walkthrough_p
text: n number data points per group. diff boost participants intervention group receive. sd standard deviation normal distributions data drawn. showdata want output dataframe containing plotted data (TRUE) (FALSE, default)? pedant want run significance test pedant mode (TRUE) (FALSE, default)? See Details.

title: Walkthrough p-values — walkthrough_p
text: Data generated normal distribution requested standard deviation. , data points randomly assigned two equal-sized groups. Data points intervention group receive boost specified diff. Finally, significance test run data. default, significance test two-sample Student's t-test. Technically, p-value test probability t-statistic larger one observed 've observed chance play, walkthrough text says probability mean difference larger one observed 've observed chance play. , use t-test approximation permutation test. Switch pedant mode want run permutation test.

title: Walkthrough p-values — walkthrough_p
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ walkthrough_p(n = 12, diff = 0.2, sd = 1.3)  # Save data and double check results dat <- walkthrough_p(n = 10, diff = 0.2, sd = 2, showdata = TRUE) t.test(score ~ group, data = dat, var.equal = TRUE)  # Run in pedant mode (= permutation test) dat <- walkthrough_p(n = 13, diff = 1, sd = 4, pedant = TRUE, showdata = TRUE) t.test(score ~ group, data = dat, var.equal = TRUE) } # }
